      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Hoagy Cunningham, Aidan Ewart, L. Smith, R. Huben, Lee Sharkey",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/edb548fe7574d99454b352ffdb61bca93c3072ba",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "One of the roadblocks to a better understanding of neural networks' internals is \\textit{polysemanticity}, where neurons appear to activate in multiple, semantically distinct contexts. Polysemanticity prevents us from identifying concise, human-understandable explanations for what neural networks are doing internally. One hypothesised cause of polysemanticity is \\textit{superposition}, where neural networks represent more features than they have neurons by assigning features to an overcomplete set of directions in activation space, rather than to individual neurons. Here, we attempt to identify those directions, using sparse autoencoders to reconstruct the internal activations of a language model. These autoencoders learn sets of sparsely activating features that are more interpretable and monosemantic than directions identified by alternative approaches, where interpretability is measured by automated methods. Moreover, we show that with our learned set of features, we can pinpoint the features that are causally responsible for counterfactual behaviour on the indirect object identification task \\citep{wang2022interpretability} to a finer degree than previous decompositions. This work indicates that it is possible to resolve superposition in language models using a scalable, unsupervised method. Our method may serve as a foundation for future mechanistic interpretability work, which we hope will enable greater model transparency and steerability.",
      "citations": 948
    },
    {
      "title": "Mechanistic Interpretability for AI Safety - A Review",
      "year": 2024,
...

      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:269293418",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Understanding AI systems' inner workings is critical for ensuring value alignment and safety. This review explores mechanistic interpretability: reverse engineering the computational mechanisms and representations learned by neural networks into human-understandable algorithms and concepts to provide a granular, causal understanding. We establish foundational concepts such as features encoding knowledge within neural activations and hypotheses about their representation and computation. We survey methodologies for causally dissecting model behaviors and assess the relevance of mechanistic interpretability to AI safety. We examine benefits in understanding, control, alignment, and risks such as capability gains and dual-use concerns. We investigate challenges surrounding scalability, automation, and comprehensive interpretation. We advocate for clarifying concepts, setting standards, and scaling techniques to handle complex models and behaviors and expand to domains such as vision and reinforcement learning.       "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/12910786da7a34c9ee26798fd81b0ed7b0e38789",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Despite rapid adoption and deployment of large language models (LLMs), the internal computations of these models remain opaque and poorly understood. In this work, we seek to understand how high-level human-interpretable features are represented within the internal neuron activations of LLMs. We train $k$-sparse linear classifiers (probes) on these internal activations to predict the presence of features in the input; by varying the value of $k$ we study the sparsity of learned representations and how this varies with model scale. With $k=1$, we localize individual neurons which are highly relevant for a particular feature, and perform a number of case studies to illustrate general properties of LLMs. In particular, we show that early layers make use of sparse combinations of neurons to represent many features in superposition, that middle layers have seemingly dedicated neurons to represent higher-level contextual features, and that increasing scale causes representational sparsity to increase on average, but there are multiple types of scaling dynamics. In all, we probe for over 100 unique features comprising 10 different categories in 7 different models spanning 70 million to 6.9 billion parameters.",
      "citations": 311
    },
    {
      "title": "Large Language Model Alignment: A Survey",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Tianhao Shen, Renren Jin, Yufei Huang, Chuang Liu, Weilong Dong, Zishan Guo, Xinwei Wu, Yan Liu, Deyi Xiong",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:262824801",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Recent years have witnessed remarkable progress made in large language models (LLMs). Such advancements, while garnering significant attention, have concurrently elicited various concerns. The potential of these models is undeniably vast; however, they may yield texts that are imprecise, misleading, or even detrimental. Consequently, it becomes paramount to employ alignment techniques to ensure these models to exhibit behaviors consistent with human values. This survey endeavors to furnish an extensive exploration of alignment methodologies designed for LLMs, in conjunction with the extant capability research in this domain. Adopting the lens of AI alignment, we categorize the prevailing methods and emergent proposals for the alignment of LLMs into outer and inner alignment. We also probe into salient issues including the models' interpretability, and potential vulnerabilities to adversarial attacks. To assess LLM alignment, we present a wide variety of benchmarks and evaluation methodologies. After discussing the state of alignment research for LLMs, we finally cast a vision toward the future, contemplating the promising avenues of research that lie ahead. Our aspiration for this survey extends beyond merely spurring research interests in this realm. We also envision bridging the gap between the AI alignment research community and the researchers engrossed in the capability exploration of LLMs for both capable and safe LLMs.",

      "abstract": "Neural networks often pack many unrelated concepts into a single neuron - a puzzling phenomenon known as 'polysemanticity' which makes interpretability much more challenging. This paper provides a toy model where polysemanticity can be fully understood, arising as a result of models storing additional sparse features in\"superposition.\"We demonstrate the existence of a phase change, a surprising connection to the geometry of uniform polytopes, and evidence of a link to adversarial examples.       "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Recent work has found that sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are an effective technique for unsupervised discovery of interpretable features in language models' (LMs) activations, by finding sparse, linear reconstructions of LM activations. We introduce the Gated Sparse Autoencoder (Gated SAE), which achieves a Pareto improvement over training with prevailing methods. In SAEs, the L1 penalty used to encourage sparsity introduces many undesirable biases, such as shrinkage -- systematic underestimation of feature activations. The key insight of Gated SAEs is to separate the functionality of (a) determining which directions to use and (b) estimating the magnitudes of those directions: this enables us to apply the L1 penalty only to the former, limiting the scope of undesirable side effects. Through training SAEs on LMs of up to 7B parameters we find that, in typical hyper-parameter ranges, Gated SAEs solve shrinkage, are similarly interpretable, and require half as many firing features to achieve comparable reconstruction fidelity.",

      "abstract": "Individual neurons in neural networks often represent a mixture of unrelated features. This phenomenon, called polysemanticity, can make interpreting neural networks more difficult and so we aim to understand its causes. We propose doing so through the lens of feature \\emph{capacity}, which is the fractional dimension each feature consumes in the embedding space. We show that in a toy model the optimal capacity allocation tends to monosemantically represent the most important features, polysemantically represent less important features (in proportion to their impact on the loss), and entirely ignore the least important features. Polysemanticity is more prevalent when the inputs have higher kurtosis or sparsity and more prevalent in some architectures than others. Given an optimal allocation of capacity, we go on to study the geometry of the embedding space.       "abstract": "Mechanistic interpretability (MI) is an emerging sub-field of interpretability that seeks to understand a neural network model by reverse-engineering its internal computations.       "abstract": "Mechanistic interpretability aims to explain what a neural network has learned at a nuts-and-bolts level. What are the fundamental primitives of neural network representations? But there are clues that neurons and their linear combinations are not the correct fundamental units of description: directions cannot describe how neural networks use nonlinearities to structure their representations. In order to find a basic unit of description that does not suffer from these problems, we zoom in beyond just directions to study the way that piecewise linear activation functions (such as ReLU) partition the activation space into numerous discrete polytopes. We call this perspective the polytope lens. The polytope lens makes concrete predictions about the behavior of neural networks, which we evaluate through experiments on both convolutional image classifiers and language models. Specifically, we show that polytopes can be used to identify monosemantic regions of activation space (while directions are not in general monosemantic) and that the density of polytope boundaries reflect semantic boundaries.       "abstract": "Superposition -- when a neural network represents more ``features'' than it has dimensions -- seems to pose a serious challenge to mechanistically interpreting current AI systems. We generalize this construction to arbitrary sparse boolean circuits of low depth, and then construct ``error correction'' layers that allow deep fully-connected networks of width $d$ to emulate circuits of width $\\tilde{O}(d^{1.5})$ and \\emph{any} polynomial depth.       "abstract": "Despite substantial efforts, neural network interpretability remains an elusive goal, with previous research failing to provide succinct explanations of most single neurons' impact on the network output.       "abstract": "We introduce combinatorial interpretability, a methodology for understanding neural computation by analyzing the combinatorial structures in the sign-based categorization of a network's weights and biases.      9→      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/edb548fe7574d99454b352ffdb61bca93c3072ba",
    10→      "relevance": 3,
    11→      "abstract": "One of the roadblocks to a better understanding of neural networks' internals is \\textit{polysemanticity}, where neurons appear to activate in multiple, semantically distinct contexts. Polysemanticity prevents us from identifying concise, human-understandable explanations for what neural networks are doing internally. One hypothesised cause of polysemanticity is \\textit{superposition}, where neural networks represent more features than they have neurons by assigning features to an overcomplete set of directions in activation space, rather than to individual neurons. Here, we attempt to identify those directions, using sparse autoencoders to reconstruct the internal activations of a language model. These autoencoders learn sets of sparsely activating features that are more interpretable and monosemantic than directions identified by alternative approaches, where interpretability is measured by automated methods. Consequently, it becomes paramount to employ alignment techniques to ensure these models to exhibit behaviors consistent with human values. python3 -c "
import json
# Read jsonl file
results = []
with open('/home/neurico/.claude/projects/-workspaces-llm-washing-machine-claude/9a14b0e0-a29e-4b48-a331-e896311f3413/tool-results/br6moocuc.txt') as f:
    content = f.read()
# Try to parse as single JSON with multiple objects
decoder = json.JSONDecoder()
pos = 0
while pos < len(content):
    content = content[pos:].lstrip()
    if not content:
        break
    obj, end = decoder.raw_decode(content)
    results.append(obj)
    pos = end

# Collect all papers
all_papers = []
for r in results:
    if 'papers' in r:
        all_papers.extend(r['papers'])

# Sort by relevance then citations
all_papers.sort(key=lambda p: (p.get('relevance',0), p.get('citations',0)), reverse=True)

# Print top 40 unique papers
seen = set()
for p in all_papers:
    t = p['title']
    if t in seen:
        continue
    seen.add(t)
    if len(seen) > 40:
        break
    print(f\"[rel={p['relevance']}|cites={p.get('citations','?')}] {t} ({p['year']})\")
    print(f\"    {p.get('url','')}\")
"

python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json

with open('/home/neurico/.claude/projects/-workspaces-llm-washing-machine-claude/9a14b0e0-a29e-4b48-a331-e896311f3413/tool-results/br6moocuc.txt') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Split on }{ pattern between JSON objects
results = []
decoder = json.JSONDecoder()
idx = 0
while idx < len(content):
    # skip whitespace
    while idx < len(content) and content[idx] in ' \t\n\r':
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(content):
        break
    try:
        obj, end_idx = decoder.raw_decode(content, idx)
        results.append(obj)
        idx = end_idx
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        idx += 1

all_papers = []
for r in results:
    if isinstance(r, dict) and 'papers' in r:
        all_papers.extend(r['papers'])

all_papers.sort(key=lambda p: (p.get('relevance',0), p.get('citations',0)), reverse=True)

seen = set()
for p in all_papers:
    t = p['title']
    if t in seen:
        continue
    seen.add(t)
    if len(seen) > 40:
        break
    print(f"[rel={p['relevance']}|cites={p.get('citations','?')}] {t} ({p['year']})")
    url = p.get('url','')
    print(f"    {url}")
PYEOF

input.description: Search for papers on compositional/compound concept representations

input.command:
python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json

# Read the second search results
results = []
decoder = json.JSONDecoder()
with open('paper_search_results/compositional_representation_compound_words_multiword_expressions_language_model_20260321_050321.jsonl') as f:
    content = f.read()
idx = 0
while idx < len(content):
    while idx < len(content) and content[idx] in ' \t\n\r':
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(content):
        break
    try:
        obj, end_idx = decoder.raw_decode(content, idx)
        results.append(obj)
        idx = end_idx
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        idx += 1

all_papers = []
for r in results:
    if isinstance(r, dict) and 'papers' in r:
        all_papers.extend(r['papers'])

all_papers.sort(key=lambda p: (p.get('relevance',0), p.get('citations',0)), reverse=True)

seen = set()
for p in all_papers:
    t = p['title']
    if t in seen:
        continue
    seen.add(t)
    if len(seen) > 25:
        break
    print(f"[rel={p['relevance']}|cites={p.get('citations','?')}] {t} ({p['year']})")
    print(f"    {p.get('url','')}")
PYEOF
ls paper_search_results/

content.text: Excellent! 610 unique papers found. Now let me select the most relevant ones for this specific research question and download them. The key papers fall into two categories: (1) mechanistic interpretability / superposition / SAEs, and (2) compositional semantics / compound word representations.

input.command:
python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json

with open('paper_search_results/all_papers.json') as f:
    papers = json.load(f)

# Select the most relevant papers for our specific research question
# Focus on: superposition, SAEs, compound concepts, compositional representations in LLMs
KEY_PAPERS = [
    # Core mechanistic interpretability
    "Toy Models of Superposition",
    "Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models",
    "Finding Neurons in a Haystack: Case Studies with Sparse Probing",
    "The Linear Representation Hypothesis and the Geometry of Large Language Models",
    "Scaling and evaluating sparse autoencoders",
    "Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Interpretable Causal Graphs in Language Models",
    "A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders",
    "Polysemanticity and Capacity in Neural Networks",
    "Language Models Implement Simple Word2Vec-style Vector Arithmetic",
    "Improving Dictionary Learning with Gated Sparse Autoencoders",
    "Rethinking Evaluation of Sparse Autoencoders through the Representation of Polysemous Words",
    "Towards Combinatorial Interpretability of Neural Computation",
    "Evaluating Synthetic Activations composed of SAE Latents in GPT-2",
    "RAVEL: Evaluating Interpretability Methods on Disentangling Language Model Representations",
    
    # Compositional semantics  
    "Assessing Phrasal Representation and Composition in Transformers",
    "An Empirical Study on Compositionality in Compound Nouns",
    "Measuring Compositionality in Representation Learning",
    "A Word Embedding Approach to Predicting the Compositionality of Multiword Expressions",
    "Still a Pain in the Neck: Evaluating Text Representations on Lexical Composition",
    "Probing for idiomaticity in vector space models",
    "Can Transformer be Too Compositional? Analysing Idiom Processing in Neural Machine Translation",
    "How Well Do Embedding Models Capture Non-compositionality? A View from Multiword Expressions",
    
    # Additional relevant
    "Understanding polysemanticity in neural networks through coding theory",
    "Do VLMs Have Bad Eyes? Diagnosing Compositional Failures via Mechanistic Interpretability",
    "Mathematical Models of Computation in Superposition",
    "Grokked Transformers are Implicit Reasoners: A Mechanistic Journey to the Edge of Generalization",
    "SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability",
]

selected = []
for p in papers:
    for key in KEY_PAPERS:
        if key.lower() in p['title'].lower():
            selected.append(p)
            break

# Also include any paper about compound concepts/compositionality with high relevance
for p in papers:
    if p in selected:
        continue
    title_lower = p['title'].lower()
    abstract_lower = p.get('abstract', '').lower()
    if p.get('relevance', 0) >= 3 and any(kw in title_lower or kw in abstract_lower for kw in [
        'compound', 'washing machine', 'multiword', 'multi-word', 'compositional representation',
        'feature absorption', 'feature splitting', 'residual stream', 'superposition'
    ]):
        if p.get('citations', 0) >= 5 or p.get('year', 0) >= 2024:
            selected.append(p)

# Deduplicate
seen = set()
final = []
for p in selected:
    if p['title'] not in seen:
        seen.add(p['title'])
        final.append(p)

print(f"Selected {len(final)} papers for download")
for i, p in enumerate(final):
    print(f"  {i+1}. {p['title']} ({p.get('year','')}, cites={p.get('citations','?')})")

# Save selection
with open('paper_search_results/selected_papers.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(final, f, indent=2)
PYEOF

content.text: Good selection of 59 papers. Now let me download the most important ones. I'll focus on the top ~20 that are most directly relevant and try to get arXiv PDFs.

input.command:
source /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/.venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json, requests, os, re, time

with open('paper_search_results/selected_papers.json') as f:
    papers = json.load(f)

# Map known papers to arXiv IDs for download
ARXIV_IDS = {
    "Toy Models of Superposition": "2209.10652",
    "Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models": "2309.08600",
    "Finding Neurons in a Haystack: Case Studies with Sparse Probing": "2305.01610",
    "The Linear Representation Hypothesis and the Geometry of Large Language Models": "2311.03658",
    "Scaling and evaluating sparse autoencoders": "2406.04093",
    "Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Interpretable Causal Graphs in Language Models": "2403.19647",
    "A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders": "2409.14507",
    "Polysemanticity and Capacity in Neural Networks": "2210.01892",
    "Language Models Implement Simple Word2Vec-style Vector Arithmetic": "2305.16130",
    "Improving Dictionary Learning with Gated Sparse Autoencoders": "2404.16014",
    "Rethinking Evaluation of Sparse Autoencoders through the Representation of Polysemous Words": "2503.06638",
    "Towards Combinatorial Interpretability of Neural Computation": "2502.15028",
    "Evaluating Synthetic Activations composed of SAE Latents in GPT-2": "2412.03120",
    "RAVEL: Evaluating Interpretability Methods on Disentangling Language Model Representations": "2402.17700",
    "Assessing Phrasal Representation and Composition in Transformers": "2010.03058",
    "Measuring Compositionality in Representation Learning": "1902.07181",
    "Still a Pain in the Neck: Evaluating Text Representations on Lexical Composition": "1902.10618",
    "Can Transformer be Too Compositional? Analysing Idiom Processing in Neural Machine Translation": "2205.07087",
    "SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability": "2501.18585",
    "Mathematical Models of Computation in Superposition": "2408.05451",
    "Understanding polysemanticity in neural networks through coding theory": "2401.12461",
    "Do VLMs Have Bad Eyes? Diagnosing Compositional Failures via Mechanistic Interpretability": "2503.01939",
    "Probing for idiomaticity in vector space models": "2104.12658",
    "Learning Multi-Level Features with Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders": "2501.03014",
    "A Systematic Search for Compound Semantics in Pretrained BERT Architectures": "2310.17975",
    "A Psycholinguistic Analysis of BERT's Representations of Compounds": "2210.01831",
    "How Is a \"Kitchen Chair\" like a \"Farm Horse\"? Exploring the Representation of Noun-Noun Compound Semantics in Transformer-based Language Models": "2211.08818",
    "Semantics of Multiword Expressions in Transformer-Based Models: A Survey": "2407.16751",
    "How do Transformer Embeddings Represent Compositions? A Functional Analysis": "2502.14671",
    "Dense SAE Latents Are Features, Not Bugs": "2502.14550",
    "OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features": "2502.13264",
}

os.makedirs('papers', exist_ok=True)

downloaded = 0
failed = []

for p in papers:
    title = p['title']
    arxiv_id = ARXIV_IDS.get(title)
    if not arxiv_id:
        continue
    
    # Create safe filename
    safe_title = re.sub(r'[^\w\s-]', '', title)[:50].strip().replace(' ', '_')
    filename = f"papers/{arxiv_id}_{safe_title}.pdf"
    
    if os.path.exists(filename) and os.path.getsize(filename) > 10000:
        print(f"  Already exists: {filename}")
        downloaded += 1
        continue
    
    url = f"https://arxiv.org/pdf/{arxiv_id}.pdf"
    print(f"Downloading: {title} ({arxiv_id})...")
    try:
        resp = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        if resp.status_code == 200 and len(resp.content) > 10000:
            with open(filename, 'wb') as f:
                f.write(resp.content)
            print(f"  OK: {len(resp.content)} bytes -> {filename}")
            downloaded += 1
        else:
            print(f"  FAILED: status={resp.status_code}, size={len(resp.content)}")
            failed.append(title)
        time.sleep(1)  # Be polite to arXiv
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"  ERROR: {e}")
        failed.append(title)

print(f"\nDownloaded: {downloaded}, Failed: {len(failed)}")
if failed:
    print("Failed papers:", failed)
PYEOF

input.command:
source /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/.venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import requests, os, time

# Additional important papers
EXTRA = {
    "2210.01831": "Psycholinguistic_Analysis_BERT_Compounds",
    "2211.08818": "Kitchen_Chair_Farm_Horse_Compound_Semantics_Transformers",
    "2502.13264": "OrtSAE_Atomic_Features",  # already downloaded
}

for arxiv_id, name in EXTRA.items():
    filename = f"papers/{arxiv_id}_{name}.pdf"
    if os.path.exists(filename) and os.path.getsize(filename) > 10000:
        print(f"Already exists: {filename}")
        continue
    # Check if already downloaded with different naming
    existing = [f for f in os.listdir('papers') if f.startswith(arxiv_id)]
    if existing:
        print(f"Already have: {existing[0]}")
        continue
    
    url = f"https://arxiv.org/pdf/{arxiv_id}.pdf"
    print(f"Downloading {arxiv_id}...")
    resp = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
    if resp.status_code == 200 and len(resp.content) > 10000:
        with open(filename, 'wb') as f:
            f.write(resp.content)
        print(f"  OK: {len(resp.content)} bytes")
    time.sleep(1)

print("\nAll papers in papers/:")
for f in sorted(os.listdir('papers')):
    if f.endswith('.pdf'):
        size = os.path.getsize(f'papers/{f}')
        print(f"  {f} ({size//1024}KB)")
PYEOF

content.text:
Read these PDF chunks of the paper "A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders" (2409.14507). This is a critical paper for research on how compound concepts like "washing machine" are stored in LLMs.

Read ALL chunks and provide detailed notes on:
1. What is feature absorption and splitting?
2. How do SAE features represent compound/complex concepts?
3. What methodology do they use?
4. What are the key findings about how features decompose concepts?
5. Any findings relevant to multi-word or compound concepts
6. Datasets and evaluation metrics used

Read these files in order:
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_001.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_002.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_003.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_004.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_005.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_006.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_007.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_008.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_009.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_010.pdf
- /workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2409.14507_A_is_for_Absorption_Studying_Feature_Splitting_and_chunk_011.pdf

This is research only - do not write any code or modify files. Just return detailed notes.

Here are detailed notes covering the full paper.

---

## Detailed Notes: "A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders" (arXiv 2409.14507, NeurIPS 2025)

**Authors:** David Chanin, James Wilken-Smith, Tomáš Dulka, Hardik Bhatnagar, Satvik Golechha, Joseph Bloom (LASR Labs et al.)

---

### 1. What is Feature Absorption and Feature Splitting?

**Feature Splitting** (prior concept, from Bricken et al. 2023):
- When SAE width increases, a general latent in a narrower SAE splits into multiple more specific latents in a wider SAE.
- Example: A latent tracking "starts with L" in a narrow SAE splits into "starts with capital L" and "starts with lowercase L" in a wider SAE.
- This is not inherently problematic — the split features are still identifiable and may be more useful for fine-grained interpretability.
- Feature splitting occurs more frequently with higher sparsity (higher L0).

**Feature Absorption** (new concept, coined in this paper):
- A problematic variant of feature splitting where a SAE latent *appears* to track a human-interpretable concept, but has seemingly arbitrary exception cases where it fails to fire.
- Instead, a more specific (sparser, often token-aligned) latent "absorbs" the feature direction and fires in its place.
- The absorbed feature direction is encoded as a small component inside the absorbing latent's decoder vector.
- Key mechanism: The SAE encoder for the main latent learns a "hole" — it fires on concept X *except* when a rarer child concept Y (which implies X) is present.

**The canonical example used throughout:**
- "starts with S" (parent, dense feature) and "short" (child, sparse/token-aligned feature).
- The word "short" starts with S, so "starts with S" should always fire when "short" fires (hierarchical implication).
- Instead, the SAE absorbs the "starts with S" direction into the "short" latent. The "starts with S" latent (6510 in Gemma Scope layer 3) silently fails to fire on `_short`, while the token-aligned "short" latent (1085) fires and covertly carries the "starts with S" signal as a small component of its decoder vector.

**Formal definition of the hierarchy:**
- f1 is parent, f2 is child: f2 ==> f1 (every time f2 fires, f1 must also fire, but f1 can fire alone).

---

### 2. How SAE Features Represent Compound/Complex Concepts

The paper reveals a fundamental structural problem in how SAEs represent concepts that exist in hierarchies:

**Ideal (interpretable) case:**
- SAE latent 1: encoder = "starts with S", decoder = "starts with S"
- SAE latent 2: encoder = "short", decoder = "short"
- Requires firing 2 latents to represent "short"

**What actually happens (absorption):**
- SAE latent 1: encoder = NOT("short") AND "starts with S", decoder = "starts with S"
- SAE latent 2: encoder = "short", decoder = "short" + "starts with S" (blended)
- Only 1 latent fires to represent "short" — more sparse, equally accurate for reconstruction, but broken for interpretability.

**Key structural consequence:** The absorbing latent's *decoder vector* becomes a superposition of the child feature direction AND a component of the parent feature direction. The absorbing latent's encoder fires when the child feature is present; the parent latent's encoder fires only when the parent is present WITHOUT the child.

**Token-aligned latents as absorbers:** The absorbing latents are frequently "token-aligned" — latents that fire on orthographic/surface variants of the same word (e.g., "short", "SHORT", "_short", "SHORT"). These are more sparse (less frequent) than general conceptual features, creating the sparsity incentive for absorption.

**Meta-SAE finding (from related work, Bussmann et al.):** Even a single latent like "Einstein" can be broken into subcomponents "German", "Physicist", "starts with E" — illustrating that SAE latents already represent blended conceptual content. Feature absorption makes this worse by making the blending covert and inconsistent.

---

### 3. Methodology

**Task used for empirical study:**
- First-letter identification: predicting the starting letter of single English tokens.
- In-context learning (ICL) prompts, e.g.: `tartan has the first letter: T` / `mirth has the first letter: M` / `dog has the first letter:`
- Residual stream activations extracted at the subject token position (e.g., `_dog`).
- Evaluation on test set (80/20 train/test split).

**Models evaluated:**
- Primary: Gemma-2-2B (base model)
- SAEs: Full set of Gemma Scope residual stream SAEs, widths 16k and 65k, all 26 layers (hundreds of SAEs total)
- Additional: Custom L1-loss SAEs trained on Qwen2 0.5B (layers 0-7) and Llama 3.2 1B (layers 0-7); TopK SAEs on Llama 3.2 1B
- Training: SAELens library, L1 coefficient 2.5, 500M tokens, single A100 GPU per SAE

**Latent identification methods:**
- Primary: cosine similarity between LR probe direction and SAE encoder directions
- Validated against: k=1 sparse probing (L1-regularized logistic regression selecting the single top-weight latent); both methods give nearly identical results

**Measuring feature splitting:**
- k-sparse probing: increase k from 1 to 15; if adding the k+1th latent causes F1 to jump by more than threshold τ=0.03, a feature split is detected.

**Measuring feature absorption rate:**
- Step 1: Find the k feature-split latents for a letter using k-sparse probing.
- Step 2: Find "false negative" tokens — those where all k SAE latents fail to fire but the LR probe correctly classifies the token.
- Step 3: Run integrated-gradients ablation on those tokens to find the most causally important latent.
- Step 4: If that latent has cosine similarity with LR probe > 0.025 AND its ablation effect is at least 1.0 larger than the second-highest latent, count it as an absorption event.
- Metric: `absorption_rate = num_absorptions / lr_probe_true_positives`
- Cap at 200 false-negative samples per letter for computational reasons.
- Metric is limited to layers 0-17 in Gemma-2-2B because after layer ~18, attention moves first-letter information from source token to prediction token, zeroing out ablation effects at the source.

**Ablation study method:**
- Insert SAE into computation path (including error term to preserve model outputs).
- Set a latent activation to 0, recompute outputs, measure logit difference.
- Metric m = g[y] - (1/|L|-1) * sum_{l != y} g[l] (correct letter logit minus mean incorrect letter logit).

**Causal verification of absorption (the "short" case study):**
- Latent 1085 has cosine similarity 0.12 with "starts with S" probe (the main latent 6510 has 0.52).
- Despite lower cosine similarity, latent 1085 fires ~5x higher magnitude on `_short`, contributing a similar absolute amount of the probe direction.
- Ablation of latent 1085 has a dramatically larger effect on model output than all other latents.
- After projecting the probe direction out of latent 1085 before ablation, its ablation effect disappears — confirming the probe component of 1085 is what causally matters.

**Toy model setup (for theoretical validation):**
- 4 true features, orthogonal, 50-dimensional, unit norm.
- f0 fires with p=0.25; f1, f2, f3 fire with p=0.05.
- Hierarchical condition: f1 only fires when f0 fires.
- SAE with 4 latents trained with SAELens, L1 coefficient 3e-5, lr 3e-4, 100M training activations.
- Cosine similarity between SAE encoder/decoder and true features used to visualize absorption.

**Analytical proof (Appendix A.2):**
- Parameterize absorption by δ ∈ [0,1]:
  - We,1 = f1 - δf2 (encoder for parent feature: suppress when child is present)
  - We,2 = f2 (encoder for child)
  - Wd,1 = f1 (decoder for parent: pure)
  - Wd,2 = f2 + δf1 (decoder for child: absorbs parent direction)
- Proposition 1: Perfect reconstruction is maintained for all δ.
- Proposition 2: Sparsity loss L_sp = p11(2-δ) + p10, with dL_sp/dδ = -p11.
- Corollary: If p11 > 0 (child co-occurs with parent with nonzero probability), increasing δ strictly decreases sparsity loss with no reconstruction cost — so gradient descent always prefers maximum absorption (δ=1).

---

### 4. Key Findings About How Features Decompose Concepts

**Finding 1: Absorption is ubiquitous.**
- Feature absorption was detected in every LLM SAE tested — all Gemma Scope sizes and layers (hundreds of SAEs), plus Qwen2 0.5B and Llama 3.2 1B SAEs, plus both L1-loss and TopK architectures.

**Finding 2: No SAE matches linear probe performance.**
- LR probe F1 score consistently exceeds the best SAE single-latent F1 score across all layers and widths.
- The performance gap is partially explained by absorption causing false negatives in otherwise good classifiers.

**Finding 3: Absorption is a fundamental consequence of hierarchical co-occurrence + sparsity, not an artifact of architecture.**
- Proven analytically (see Appendix A.2) and demonstrated empirically in toy models.
- TopK SAEs (which enforce sparsity via k constraint, no L1 term) also exhibit absorption, confirming it is not specific to L1 regularization.

**Finding 4: Changing SAE width or sparsity does not solve absorption.**
- Wider SAEs (65k vs 16k) show higher absorption rates.
- Lower L0 (sparser) SAEs show higher absorption rates.
- Higher L0 (less sparse) SAEs have lower absorption but worse precision and more polysemanticity.
- There is a fundamental precision-recall tradeoff: low L0 → high precision/low recall; high L0 → low precision/high recall. No setting resolves this.

**Finding 5: Absorption creates gerrymandered latents.**
- The encoder direction of the parent-feature latent develops a "hole": it actively suppresses activation when the child feature is present (encoder = "starts with S AND NOT short"). This is not just a failure to fire — the encoder is explicitly gerrymandered to exclude the child case.
- The decoder direction of the child latent contains a small but causally significant component of the parent direction.

**Finding 6: Partial absorption exists.**
- When parent feature magnitude varies, the parent latent does not always turn off completely — it fires very weakly when the child is also active, and turns off only if the parent feature magnitude drops below a threshold.
- Even imperfect co-occurrence (feature 1 fires with feature 0 95% of the time, but can fire alone 5% of the time) still produces partial absorption.

**Finding 7: Absorption rate increases with sparsity and SAE width.**
- Mean absorption rates up to ~30-35% are observed in higher-L0 Gemma Scope SAEs.
- The absorption metric is a conservative lower bound — it only counts cases with one dominant absorbing latent that fully suppresses the main latent(s).

---

### 5. Findings Relevant to Multi-Word / Compound Concepts

The paper's canonical example is directly relevant to compound/multi-token concepts, though framed at the single-token level:

**The "short" / "starts with S" example is a direct token-level compound concept case:**
- "short" is a semantically meaningful token that participates in a feature hierarchy: it simultaneously carries the semantic concept of brevity ("short") AND the orthographic property of beginning with S.
- The SAE cannot cleanly separate these two aspects because they always co-occur in the token `_short`.

**The token-alignment phenomenon:**
- Specific tokens ("short", "SHORT", "_short", "_shorts") have their own token-aligned latents that absorb more general features.
- This is directly analogous to how a compound like "washing machine" would form a specific token-aligned or concept-aligned latent that could absorb the directions of more general parent concepts ("machine", "appliance", "laundry", etc.).

**Meta-SAE example (from related work cited):**
- Bussmann et al. demonstrate that the "Einstein" SAE latent decomposes into "German", "Physicist", "starts with E" — showing compound concepts are already blended in SAE latents, and absorption makes this blend covert and unreliable.

**Implication for compound/multi-word concept research:**
- If a concept like "washing machine" has a dedicated SAE latent, that latent's decoder will contain components of parent concepts (e.g., "machine", "appliance", "cleaning"), but the parent concept latents will silently fail to fire on tokens that evoke "washing machine" specifically.
- The more semantically specific and less frequent a concept is, the more it will absorb from parent concepts.
- Conversely, the more general the parent feature (e.g., "machine-related"), the more likely it is to be absorbed by multiple child concepts simultaneously — causing its SAE latent to have systematically low recall across many specific tokens.
- The paper gives a concrete example of this logic in the conclusion: "If a dense feature like 'starts with letter D' always co-occurs with a more sparse feature like 'dogs', the SAE can increase sparsity by absorbing the 'starts with D' feature into a 'dogs' latent."

---

### 6. Datasets and Evaluation Metrics

**Dataset:**
- Vocabulary of single English tokens containing characters a-z, A-Z (and optional leading space).
- Each token tested via ICL prompts eliciting first-letter identification.
- Gemma-2-2B evaluated at residual stream activation position of the subject token.
- 80/20 train/test split for LR probe training.
- SAE training for custom models: 500M tokens each.

**Primary task metric:**
- Logit difference: m = g[y] - (1/|L|-1) * sum_{l ≠ y} g[l]
- Measures model's confidence in correct starting letter vs. mean of all incorrect letters.
- Also tested: max-logit version m_max = g[y] - max_{l ≠ y} g[l]; both versions give qualitatively identical absorption rate curves.

**Classification metrics on SAE latents:**
- Precision, Recall, F1 score for binary classification (does this letter start this token?)
- F1 used as main metric to balance precision/recall tradeoff.

**Absorption rate metric:**
- `absorption_rate = num_absorptions / lr_probe_true_positives`
- Uses integrated-gradients ablation to verify causal impact of absorbing latent.
- Thresholds: cosine similarity with probe > 0.025; dominant ablation effect leading by at least 1.0.
- Acknowledged as a conservative lower bound (under-estimates true absorption).
- Valid only for layers where the source token causally mediates output (layers 0-17 in Gemma-2-2B).

**Feature splitting metric:**
- k-sparse probing with k from 1 to 15.
- A feature split is detected when increasing k by 1 raises F1 by > τ = 0.03.

**Intervention/editing metric:**
- "Edit out" score: decrease in true first letter probability after zeroing the corresponding SAE latent.
- "Edit in" score: increase in new (random) letter probability after boosting the corresponding SAE latent.
- Best editing performance at L0 75-150 (16k SAEs), layers 7-9.

---

### Summary of Implications for "Washing Machine" Research

This paper is highly relevant to the question of how compound concepts like "washing machine" are stored:

1. **Hierarchical structure matters critically.** "Washing machine" implies "machine", "appliance", "electric device", etc. Each of these hierarchical parent features will tend to be absorbed by the "washing machine" latent to the extent they co-occur.

2. **Token-aligned vs. concept-level latents.** "Washing machine" may appear as a token-aligned latent (firing on "_washing", "machine", " washing machine" as a bigram, etc.) and will absorb the directional components of parent features, making those parent latents silently fail.

3. **SAE latents for compound concepts are unreliable classifiers.** Even if you find a "washing machine" latent with high precision (correct when it fires), its recall will be suppressed because: (a) more specific sub-concepts of "washing machine" may absorb it, and (b) it may absorb from more general parent concepts making those unreliable in turn.

4. **The representation is distributed and covert.** A compound concept like "washing machine" is not stored in a single clean latent. It participates in a web of hierarchical absorptions, where: the specific latent's decoder contains blended directions of parent concepts; the parent concept latents have gerrymandered encoders that exclude the "washing machine" case; and ablation studies are needed to confirm the causal role of each latent.

5. **No hyperparameter tuning will fully solve this.** Varying SAE width or sparsity (L0) cannot eliminate absorption — it only shifts the precision/recall tradeoff. The problem is structural and requires new architectural or training approaches.

Here are detailed notes covering the full paper.

---

## Detailed Notes: "A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders" (arXiv 2409.14507, NeurIPS 2025)

**Authors:** David Chanin, James Wilken-Smith, Tomáš Dulka, Hardik Bhatnagar, Satvik Golechha, Joseph Bloom (LASR Labs et al.)

---

### 1. What is Feature Absorption and Feature Splitting?

**Feature Splitting** (prior concept, from Bricken et al. 2023):
- When SAE width increases, a general latent in a narrower SAE splits into multiple more specific latents in a wider SAE.
- Example: A latent tracking "starts with L" in a narrow SAE splits into "starts with capital L" and "starts with lowercase L" in a wider SAE.
- This is not inherently problematic — the split features are still identifiable and may be more useful for fine-grained interpretability.
- Feature splitting occurs more frequently with higher sparsity (higher L0).

**Feature Absorption** (new concept, coined in this paper):
- A problematic variant of feature splitting where a SAE latent *appears* to track a human-interpretable concept, but has seemingly arbitrary exception cases where it fails to fire.
- Instead, a more specific (sparser, often token-aligned) latent "absorbs" the feature direction and fires in its place.
- The absorbed feature direction is encoded as a small component inside the absorbing latent's decoder vector.
- Key mechanism: The SAE encoder for the main latent learns a "hole" — it fires on concept X *except* when a rarer child concept Y (which implies X) is present.

**The canonical example used throughout:**
- "starts with S" (parent, dense feature) and "short" (child, sparse/token-aligned feature).
- The word "short" starts with S, so "starts with S" should always fire when "short" fires (hierarchical implication).
- Instead, the SAE absorbs the "starts with S" direction into the "short" latent. The "starts with S" latent (6510 in Gemma Scope layer 3) silently fails to fire on `_short`, while the token-aligned "short" latent (1085) fires and covertly carries the "starts with S" signal as a small component of its decoder vector.

**Formal definition of the hierarchy:**
- f1 is parent, f2 is child: f2 ==> f1 (every time f2 fires, f1 must also fire, but f1 can fire alone).

---

### 2. How SAE Features Represent Compound/Complex Concepts

The paper reveals a fundamental structural problem in how SAEs represent concepts that exist in hierarchies:

**Ideal (interpretable) case:**
- SAE latent 1: encoder = "starts with S", decoder = "starts with S"
- SAE latent 2: encoder = "short", decoder = "short"
- Requires firing 2 latents to represent "short"

**What actually happens (absorption):**
- SAE latent 1: encoder = NOT("short") AND "starts with S", decoder = "starts with S"
- SAE latent 2: encoder = "short", decoder = "short" + "starts with S" (blended)
- Only 1 latent fires to represent "short" — more sparse, equally accurate for reconstruction, but broken for interpretability.

**Key structural consequence:** The absorbing latent's *decoder vector* becomes a superposition of the child feature direction AND a component of the parent feature direction. The absorbing latent's encoder fires when the child feature is present; the parent latent's encoder fires only when the parent is present WITHOUT the child.

**Token-aligned latents as absorbers:** The absorbing latents are frequently "token-aligned" — latents that fire on orthographic/surface variants of the same word (e.g., "short", "SHORT", "_short", "SHORT"). These are more sparse (less frequent) than general conceptual features, creating the sparsity incentive for absorption.

**Meta-SAE finding (from related work, Bussmann et al.):** Even a single latent like "Einstein" can be broken into subcomponents "German", "Physicist", "starts with E" — illustrating that SAE latents already represent blended conceptual content. Feature absorption makes this worse by making the blending covert and inconsistent.

---

### 3. Methodology

**Task used for empirical study:**
- First-letter identification: predicting the starting letter of single English tokens.
- In-context learning (ICL) prompts, e.g.: `tartan has the first letter: T` / `mirth has the first letter: M` / `dog has the first letter:`
- Residual stream activations extracted at the subject token position (e.g., `_dog`).
- Evaluation on test set (80/20 train/test split).

**Models evaluated:**
- Primary: Gemma-2-2B (base model)
- SAEs: Full set of Gemma Scope residual stream SAEs, widths 16k and 65k, all 26 layers (hundreds of SAEs total)
- Additional: Custom L1-loss SAEs trained on Qwen2 0.5B (layers 0-7) and Llama 3.2 1B (layers 0-7); TopK SAEs on Llama 3.2 1B
- Training: SAELens library, L1 coefficient 2.5, 500M tokens, single A100 GPU per SAE

**Latent identification methods:**
- Primary: cosine similarity between LR probe direction and SAE encoder directions
- Validated against: k=1 sparse probing (L1-regularized logistic regression selecting the single top-weight latent); both methods give nearly identical results

**Measuring feature splitting:**
- k-sparse probing: increase k from 1 to 15; if adding the k+1th latent causes F1 to jump by more than threshold τ=0.03, a feature split is detected.

**Measuring feature absorption rate:**
- Step 1: Find the k feature-split latents for a letter using k-sparse probing.
- Step 2: Find "false negative" tokens — those where all k SAE latents fail to fire but the LR probe correctly classifies the token.
- Step 3: Run integrated-gradients ablation on those tokens to find the most causally important latent.
- Step 4: If that latent has cosine similarity with LR probe > 0.025 AND its ablation effect is at least 1.0 larger than the second-highest latent, count it as an absorption event.
- Metric: `absorption_rate = num_absorptions / lr_probe_true_positives`
- Cap at 200 false-negative samples per letter for computational reasons.
- Metric is limited to layers 0-17 in Gemma-2-2B because after layer ~18, attention moves first-letter information from source token to prediction token, zeroing out ablation effects at the source.

**Ablation study method:**
- Insert SAE into computation path (including error term to preserve model outputs).
- Set a latent activation to 0, recompute outputs, measure logit difference.
- Metric m = g[y] - (1/|L|-1) * sum_{l != y} g[l] (correct letter logit minus mean incorrect letter logit).

**Causal verification of absorption (the "short" case study):**
- Latent 1085 has cosine similarity 0.12 with "starts with S" probe (the main latent 6510 has 0.52).
- Despite lower cosine similarity, latent 1085 fires ~5x higher magnitude on `_short`, contributing a similar absolute amount of the probe direction.
- Ablation of latent 1085 has a dramatically larger effect on model output than all other latents.
- After projecting the probe direction out of latent 1085 before ablation, its ablation effect disappears — confirming the probe component of 1085 is what causally matters.

**Toy model setup (for theoretical validation):**
- 4 true features, orthogonal, 50-dimensional, unit norm.
- f0 fires with p=0.25; f1, f2, f3 fire with p=0.05.
- Hierarchical condition: f1 only fires when f0 fires.
- SAE with 4 latents trained with SAELens, L1 coefficient 3e-5, lr 3e-4, 100M training activations.
- Cosine similarity between SAE encoder/decoder and true features used to visualize absorption.

**Analytical proof (Appendix A.2):**
- Parameterize absorption by δ ∈ [0,1]:
  - We,1 = f1 - δf2 (encoder for parent feature: suppress when child is present)
  - We,2 = f2 (encoder for child)
  - Wd,1 = f1 (decoder for parent: pure)
  - Wd,2 = f2 + δf1 (decoder for child: absorbs parent direction)
- Proposition 1: Perfect reconstruction is maintained for all δ.
- Proposition 2: Sparsity loss L_sp = p11(2-δ) + p10, with dL_sp/dδ = -p11.
- Corollary: If p11 > 0 (child co-occurs with parent with nonzero probability), increasing δ strictly decreases sparsity loss with no reconstruction cost — so gradient descent always prefers maximum absorption (δ=1).

---

### 4. Key Findings About How Features Decompose Concepts

**Finding 1: Absorption is ubiquitous.**
- Feature absorption was detected in every LLM SAE tested — all Gemma Scope sizes and layers (hundreds of SAEs), plus Qwen2 0.5B and Llama 3.2 1B SAEs, plus both L1-loss and TopK architectures.

**Finding 2: No SAE matches linear probe performance.**
- LR probe F1 score consistently exceeds the best SAE single-latent F1 score across all layers and widths.
- The performance gap is partially explained by absorption causing false negatives in otherwise good classifiers.

**Finding 3: Absorption is a fundamental consequence of hierarchical co-occurrence + sparsity, not an artifact of architecture.**
- Proven analytically (see Appendix A.2) and demonstrated empirically in toy models.
- TopK SAEs (which enforce sparsity via k constraint, no L1 term) also exhibit absorption, confirming it is not specific to L1 regularization.

**Finding 4: Changing SAE width or sparsity does not solve absorption.**
- Wider SAEs (65k vs 16k) show higher absorption rates.
- Lower L0 (sparser) SAEs show higher absorption rates.
- Higher L0 (less sparse) SAEs have lower absorption but worse precision and more polysemanticity.
- There is a fundamental precision-recall tradeoff: low L0 → high precision/low recall; high L0 → low precision/high recall. No setting resolves this.

**Finding 5: Absorption creates gerrymandered latents.**
- The encoder direction of the parent-feature latent develops a "hole": it actively suppresses activation when the child feature is present (encoder = "starts with S AND NOT short"). This is not just a failure to fire — the encoder is explicitly gerrymandered to exclude the child case.
- The decoder direction of the child latent contains a small but causally significant component of the parent direction.

**Finding 6: Partial absorption exists.**
- When parent feature magnitude varies, the parent latent does not always turn off completely — it fires very weakly when the child is also active, and turns off only if the parent feature magnitude drops below a threshold.
- Even imperfect co-occurrence (feature 1 fires with feature 0 95% of the time, but can fire alone 5% of the time) still produces partial absorption.

**Finding 7: Absorption rate increases with sparsity and SAE width.**
- Mean absorption rates up to ~30-35% are observed in higher-L0 Gemma Scope SAEs.
- The absorption metric is a conservative lower bound — it only counts cases with one dominant absorbing latent that fully suppresses the main latent(s).

---

### 5. Findings Relevant to Multi-Word / Compound Concepts

The paper's canonical example is directly relevant to compound/multi-token concepts, though framed at the single-token level:

**The "short" / "starts with S" example is a direct token-level compound concept case:**
- "short" is a semantically meaningful token that participates in a feature hierarchy: it simultaneously carries the semantic concept of brevity ("short") AND the orthographic property of beginning with S.
- The SAE cannot cleanly separate these two aspects because they always co-occur in the token `_short`.

- This is directly analogous to how a compound like "washing machine" would form a specific token-aligned or concept-aligned latent that could absorb the directions of more general parent concepts ("machine", "appliance", "laundry", etc.).

- Bussmann et al. demonstrate that the "Einstein" SAE latent decomposes into "German", "Physicist", "starts with E" — showing compound concepts are already blended in SAE latents, and absorption makes this blend covert and unreliable.

- If a concept like "washing machine" has a dedicated SAE latent, that latent's decoder will contain components of parent concepts (e.g., "machine", "appliance", "cleaning"), but the parent concept latents will silently fail to fire on tokens that evoke "washing machine" specifically.
- The more semantically specific and less frequent a concept is, the more it will absorb from parent concepts.
- Conversely, the more general the parent feature (e.g., "machine-related"), the more likely it is to be absorbed by multiple child concepts simultaneously — causing its SAE latent to have systematically low recall across many specific tokens.
- The paper gives a concrete example of this logic in the conclusion: "If a dense feature like 'starts with letter D' always co-occurs with a more sparse feature like 'dogs', the SAE can increase sparsity by absorbing the 'starts with D' feature into a 'dogs' latent."

---

### 6. Datasets and Evaluation Metrics

**Dataset:**
- Vocabulary of single English tokens containing characters a-z, A-Z (and optional leading space).
- Each token tested via ICL prompts eliciting first-letter identification.
- Gemma-2-2B evaluated at residual stream activation position of the subject token.
- 80/20 train/test split for LR probe training.
- SAE training for custom models: 500M tokens each.

**Primary task metric:**
- Logit difference: m = g[y] - (1/|L|-1) * sum_{l ≠ y} g[l]
- Measures model's confidence in correct starting letter vs. mean of all incorrect letters.
- Also tested: max-logit version m_max = g[y] - max_{l ≠ y} g[l]; both versions give qualitatively identical absorption rate curves.

**Classification metrics on SAE latents:**
- Precision, Recall, F1 score for binary classification (does this letter start this token?)

- `absorption_rate = num_absorptions / lr_probe_true_positives`

- Thresholds: cosine similarity with probe > 0.025; dominant ablation effect leading by at least 1.0.

**Feature splitting metric:**

- A feature split is detected when increasing k by 1 raises F1 by > τ = 0.03.

- "Edit in" score: increase in new (random) letter probability after boosting the corresponding SAE latent.
- Best editing performance at L0 75-150 (16k SAEs), layers 7-9.

---

The PDF files on disk were read successfully, but the files appear to contain different papers than what their filenames suggest. The filename `2310.17975_A_Systematic_Search_for_Compound_Semantics_in_Pret` resolves to a 21-cm cosmology paper, not a compound semantics paper, and `2502.14671_How_do_Transformer_Embeddings_Represent_Compositio` resolves to the fMRI/XAI brain alignment paper. The first set of files (2211.08818) resolved to a spider web chemistry paper, not a transformer/NLP paper.

Here is what I can offer based on what was actually in the files:

**What was in the files that ARE relevant to the washing machine / LLM research:**

From `2502.14671` (Rahimi et al., "Explanations of Large Language Models Explain Language Representations in the Brain"), which, while not the requested paper on compositional embeddings, does contain material relevant to understanding how LLMs represent linguistic content:

- Uses XAI attribution methods (Gradient Norm, Gradient x Input, Integrated Gradients, Erasure) to probe how LLMs weight preceding words for next-word prediction
- Tests GPT-2, Llama 2, Phi-2 against fMRI data from 147 participants listening to stories
- Finds early model layers align with early auditory/language processing regions (Heschl's gyrus, STG), later layers with higher-order regions (IFG, angular gyrus)
- Strong Pearson correlation (r = 0.97) between a layer's importance for next-word prediction and its brain alignment
- Early layers most influence content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives); later layers dominate function words
- Attribution methods outperform raw activations in early language areas despite being much lower-dimensional (10 features vs. 768)

This has some peripheral relevance — it speaks to how LLMs distribute representation of content words across layers — but it is not about compound noun semantics.

**Recommendation:** The three target papers need to be re-downloaded. The correct arXiv papers are:

- **2211.08818**: Bankole et al. (or similar authors) on noun-noun compound semantics in transformers — the "kitchen chair / farm horse" paper
- **2310.17975**: The compound semantics in pretrained BERT architectures paper

# Try to extract text using zlib decompression on PDF streams
import zlib
import re

def extract_pdf_text(filepath):
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read()
    
    # Find compressed streams
    texts = []
    pos = 0
    while True:
        stream_start = data.find(b'stream\r\n', pos)
        if stream_start == -1:
            stream_start = data.find(b'stream\n', pos)
        if stream_start == -1:
            break
        
        stream_end = data.find(b'endstream', stream_start)
        if stream_end == -1:
            break
        
        # Get the stream content
        content_start = stream_start + 7
        if data[stream_start:stream_start+8] == b'stream\r\n':
            content_start = stream_start + 8
        
        stream_data = data[content_start:stream_end]
        
        try:
            decompressed = zlib.decompress(stream_data)
            # Look for text in the decompressed data
            text_parts = re.findall(rb'\(((?:[^()\\]|\\.)*)\)\s*Tj', decompressed)
            text_parts2 = re.findall(rb'\[((?:[^[\]]*\([^)]*\)[^[\]]*)*)\]\s*TJ', decompressed)
            
            for part in text_parts:
                try:
                    decoded = part.decode('latin-1')
                    if len(decoded.strip()) > 3:
                        texts.append(decoded)
                except:
                    pass
        except:
            pass
        
        pos = stream_end + 9
    
    return ' '.join(texts)

result = extract_pdf_text('/workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2502.15028_Towards_Combinatorial_Interpretability_of_Neural_C_chunk_001.pdf')
print("Paper 1 chunk 1 text (first 3000 chars):")
print(result[:3000])
EOF

print(f'Found {len(matches)} compressed streams')

        dec = zlib.decompress(m)
        # Look for BT...ET blocks (text blocks in PDF)
        bt_blocks = re.findall(rb'BT(.*?)ET', dec, re.DOTALL)

content.text:
The situation is fully confirmed. Both the source PDF file `2502.15028_Towards_Combinatorial_Interpretability_of_Neural_C.pdf` AND all its chunk files contain the AGN astrophysics paper. The file was apparently downloaded from a wrong arXiv preprint. The arXiv ID 2502.15028 in the astrophysics subject area (`astro-ph.HE`) corresponds to "A New Framework for AGN Accretion and Jet Feedback" by Lu & Ricker — the paper that actually appeared at arXiv ID 2502.15028. There is no ML interpretability paper at that ID.

Similarly, 2412.03120 at `cs.DS` is the Sinkhorn/optimal transport paper by Watanabe & Isobe, not an SAE interpretability paper.

The paper titles in the filenames ("Towards Combinatorial Interpretability of Neural Computation" and "Evaluating Synthetic Activations composed of SAE Latents in GPT-2") do not correspond to those arXiv IDs. These appear to be the correct intended paper titles, but either the wrong arXiv IDs were used when downloading, or the files were somehow mixed up during the download/chunking process.

---

**Summary of findings:**

The task cannot be completed as requested. There is a fundamental data problem with the files provided:

**Paper 1** — Files at `/workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2502.15028_Towards_Combinatorial_Interpretability_of_Neural_C_chunk_00*.pdf`
- Actual content: "A New Framework for AGN Accretion and Jet Feedback in Numerical Simulations" by Ying-He Celeste Lu & Paul Ricker (astrophysics, Cambridge/UIUC)
- arXiv ID confirmed internally: `2502.15028 [astro-ph.HE]`
- This is a paper about galaxy cluster simulations, AGN jets, and black hole accretion — completely unrelated to neural network interpretability or SAE features

**Paper 2** — Files at `/workspaces/llm-washing-machine-claude/papers/pages/2412.03120_Evaluating_Synthetic_Activations_composed_of_SAE_L_chunk_00*.pdf`
- Actual content: "Sinkhorn Algorithm for Sequentially Composed Optimal Transports" by Kazuki Watanabe & Noboru Isobe (National Institute of Informatics, Tokyo)
